definition: The FX/dollar channel is a critical and systematically underappreciated mediator of commodity-inflation transmission. Because commodities are dollar-invoiced (Gopinath dominant currency paradigm), the dollar's trajectory determines realized passthrough for every non-US country. DM exchange rate passthrough to CPI is 0.03-0.08; EM ERPT is 0.15-0.40.
evidence: Japan 2022: 30% JPY depreciation transformed moderate global energy shock into 4.3% CPI (40-year high)
Turkey/Egypt/Pakistan 2022: inflation outcomes almost entirely explained by FX channel, not commodity prices alone
BIS trade-weighted dollar index explains 30-40% of cross-country CPI variance for identical commodity shocks
10% USD depreciation adds ~0.5-1.0pp to US headline CPI
Fed tightening strengthens dollar, partially self-hedging US but amplifying passthrough for everyone else — the commodity-FX doom loop (EUR/USD parity Sept 2022)
open_questions: Will dedollarization of commodity invoicing meaningfully alter this channel?
How does the energy transition (shift from dollar-denominated oil to less-financialized minerals) affect FX mediation?
Is the DM/EM ERPT gap widening or narrowing?
